systems.name: 3DO Interactive Multiplayer
systems.description: A 32-bit home video game console developed by The 3DO Company, featuring CD-ROM based games and multimedia capabilities.

systems.name: Commodore Amiga
systems.description: A family of personal computers by Commodore known for advanced graphics and sound capabilities, popular in Europe during the late 1980s.

systems.name: Amstrad CPC
systems.description: A series of 8-bit home computers popular in the UK and Europe, known for their built-in cassette or disk drives.

systems.name: Apple II
systems.description: Apple's influential 8-bit personal computer series, one of the first mass-produced microcomputers and a staple in American schools.

systems.name: Apple IIGS
systems.description: The enhanced Apple II model featuring a 16-bit processor and improved graphics and sound, bridging the gap to the Macintosh era.

systems.name: Arcade
systems.description: Coin-operated arcade game machines, covering hardware from the golden age of arcade gaming through modern systems.

systems.name: Atari 2600
systems.description: Atari's iconic cartridge-based home console that popularized the concept of bringing arcade games into the living room.

systems.name: Atari 5200
systems.description: Atari's second-generation home console designed to compete with the Mattel Intellivision and Coleco ColecoVision.

systems.name: Atari 7800 ProSystem
systems.description: Atari's backward-compatible 8-bit console, compatible with Atari 2600 cartridges and featuring improved graphics.

systems.name: Atari 800
systems.description: Atari's 8-bit home computer series, offering strong graphics and sound for its era and a large software library.

systems.name: Atari Jaguar
systems.description: Atari's 64-bit home game console, one of the last consoles produced by Atari before the company exited the hardware market.

systems.name: Atari Jaguar CD
systems.description: The CD-ROM add-on for the Atari Jaguar console, expanding the game library with FMV and larger titles.

systems.name: Atari Lynx
systems.description: Atari's color handheld game console, the first handheld with a color LCD screen.

systems.name: Atari ST
systems.description: Atari's 16-bit personal computer, popular among musicians for its built-in MIDI ports and in Europe as a home computer.

systems.name: Atari XE
systems.description: The XE series of Atari 8-bit computers, designed as a cost-reduced continuation of the Atari 800XL.

systems.name: Commodore 64
systems.description: Commodore's iconic 8-bit home computer, the best-selling single computer model of all time with a vast game library.

systems.name: Philips CD-i
systems.description: Philips' CD-i multimedia player and gaming console, known for its FMV-based games and multimedia capabilities.

systems.name: Commodore CDTV
systems.description: Commodore's CD-ROM multimedia player based on the Amiga platform, targeting the home entertainment market.

systems.name: Coleco ColecoVision
systems.description: Coleco's second-generation home console known for near-arcade-quality ports and an add-on expansion module.

systems.name: DOS (PC)
systems.description: MS-DOS, Microsoft's disk operating system that powered IBM-compatible PCs through the 1980s and early 1990s.

systems.name: Sega Dreamcast
systems.description: Sega's final home console, featuring online gaming capabilities and a library of beloved titles.

systems.name: Nintendo Famicom Disk System
systems.description: The Famicom Disk System, Nintendo's floppy disk add-on for the Famicom offering rewritable game storage.

systems.name: Adobe Flash
systems.description: Adobe Flash, a multimedia platform used to create animations, games, and interactive web content.

systems.name: Nintendo Game and Watch
systems.description: Nintendo's line of handheld electronic games, the precursor to the Game Boy and an icon of early portable gaming.

systems.name: Sega Game Gear
systems.description: Sega's portable gaming device, a backlit color handheld that competed directly with Nintendo's Game Boy.

systems.name: Nintendo Game Boy
systems.description: Nintendo's original Game Boy, the monochrome handheld that dominated portable gaming through the 1990s.

systems.name: Nintendo Game Boy Advance
systems.description: Nintendo's 32-bit handheld console, offering home console quality games in a portable format.

systems.name: Nintendo Game Boy Color
systems.description: The Game Boy Color, Nintendo's updated handheld featuring a color screen while maintaining backward compatibility.

systems.name: Nintendo GameCube
systems.description: Nintendo's GameCube, a compact 128-bit console known for its unique disc format and strong first-party titles.

systems.name: Sega Genesis / Mega Drive
systems.description: Sega's 16-bit home console, known as the Mega Drive outside North America, iconic for its Sonic the Hedgehog games.

systems.name: Mattel Electronics Intellivision
systems.description: Mattel Electronics' second-generation home console, the first serious competitor to the Atari 2600.

systems.name: LaserDisc Games
systems.description: LaserDisc-based arcade games, known for their full-motion video gameplay pioneered by Dragon's Lair.

systems.name: Apple Macintosh
systems.description: Apple's iconic personal computer line featuring a graphical user interface, launched in 1984.

systems.name: Sega Master System
systems.description: Sega's 8-bit home console, a strong competitor to the NES in Europe and Brazil.

systems.name: Sega Mega-CD
systems.description: Sega's CD-ROM add-on for the Mega Drive, expanding the platform with FMV games and enhanced audio.

systems.name: MSX1
systems.description: The original MSX standard, an 8-bit home computer platform with a vast software library.

systems.name: MSX2
systems.description: The enhanced MSX standard featuring improved graphics modes and memory management.

systems.name: MSX Turbo R
systems.description: The final MSX standard featuring a faster R800 processor, released only in Japan.

systems.name: Nintendo 3DS
systems.description: Nintendo's glasses-free 3D handheld console, the successor to the DS with a large game library.

systems.name: Nintendo 64
systems.description: Nintendo's 64-bit cartridge-based console, home to landmark titles like Super Mario 64 and The Legend of Zelda: Ocarina of Time.

systems.name: Sega NAOMI
systems.description: Sega's arcade board based on Dreamcast hardware, used for many of Sega's late-90s and early-2000s arcade titles.

systems.name: Sega NAOMI 2
systems.description: The enhanced successor to the Sega NAOMI board, featuring improved polygon performance.

systems.name: Nintendo DS
systems.description: Nintendo's dual-screen handheld with a touchscreen, one of the best-selling game consoles of all time.

systems.name: SNK Neo Geo
systems.description: SNK's high-powered arcade and home platform, famous for its premium price and exceptional fighting games.

systems.name: SNK Neo Geo CD
systems.description: The CD-ROM version of SNK's Neo Geo platform, offering the same arcade titles at a lower cartridge cost.

systems.name: Nintendo Entertainment System / Nintendo Family Computer
systems.description: Nintendo's 8-bit home console that revitalized the video game industry after the 1983 crash.

systems.name: Nokia N-Gage
systems.description: Nokia's mobile phone and gaming device hybrid, attempting to combine a smartphone with a dedicated handheld.

systems.name: SNK Neo Geo Pocket
systems.description: SNK's Neo Geo Pocket, a monochrome handheld featuring SNK's classic fighting game franchises.

systems.name: SNK Neo Geo Pocket Color
systems.description: SNK's Neo Geo Pocket Color, the color upgrade to the NGP with a strong library of fighting and puzzle games.

systems.name: NEC PC-8800 Series
systems.description: NEC's PC-88 series of Japanese home computers, popular for RPGs and visual novels in the 1980s.

systems.name: NEC PC-9800 Series
systems.description: NEC's PC-98 series of Japanese computers, dominant in Japan through the 1980s and 1990s with a rich game library.

systems.name: NEC PC Engine
systems.description: NEC's PC Engine, known as TurboGrafx-16 in North America, a compact console with a strong arcade port library.

systems.name: NEC PC-FX
systems.description: NEC's 32-bit successor to the PC Engine, released only in Japan and known for its FMV capabilities.

systems.name: Nintendo Pokémon Mini
systems.description: Nintendo's tiny handheld game console designed exclusively for Pokémon-themed games.

systems.name: Sony PlayStation 2
systems.description: Sony's PlayStation 2, the best-selling home console of all time with an enormous game library.

systems.name: Sony PlayStation 3
systems.description: Sony's PlayStation 3, featuring Blu-ray playback, online gaming via PSN, and the CELL processor architecture.

systems.name: Sony PlayStation 4
systems.description: Sony's PlayStation 4, a powerful eighth-generation console with a large library of modern games.

systems.name: Sony PlayStation Portable
systems.description: Sony's PlayStation Portable, a powerful handheld offering near-PS2 quality gaming on the go.

systems.name: Sony PlayStation Vita
systems.description: Sony's PlayStation Vita, a handheld with dual analog sticks and an OLED screen with a strong indie game library.

systems.name: Sony PlayStation
systems.description: Sony's original PlayStation, the console that shifted gaming toward 3D polygon-based graphics and CD-ROM media.

systems.name: Sega Saturn
systems.description: Sega's 32-bit home console featuring a dual-CPU design and an impressive library of 2D and 3D games.

systems.name: ScummVM Game Engine
systems.description: An engine for running classic point-and-click adventure games from LucasArts, Sierra, and other publishers.

systems.name: Sega 32X
systems.description: Sega's 32-bit add-on for the Mega Drive/Genesis, designed to extend the life of the platform.

systems.name: Sega CD
systems.description: Sega's CD-ROM add-on for the Genesis, known as the Mega-CD in other regions.

systems.name: Sega SG-1000
systems.description: Sega's first home console, released in Japan on the same day as the Famicom.

systems.name: Super Nintendo / Super Famicom
systems.description: Nintendo's Super Nintendo Entertainment System, a 16-bit console home to iconic RPGs, platformers, and fighting games.

systems.name: Nintendo Switch
systems.description: Nintendo's hybrid home and portable console featuring detachable Joy-Con controllers and a large game library.

systems.name: Symbian
systems.description: Nokia's Symbian mobile operating system, which powered many smartphones in the early 2000s.

systems.name: Namco-Sega-Nintendo Triforce
systems.description: A Sega, Nintendo, and Namco joint arcade board based on GameCube hardware, used in games like Mario Kart Arcade GP.

systems.name: Commodore VIC-20
systems.description: Commodore's VIC-20, the first home computer to sell one million units and a precursor to the Commodore 64.

systems.name: Nintendo Virtual Boy
systems.description: Nintendo's ill-fated stereoscopic 3D tabletop console, displaying monochrome red graphics.

systems.name: Nintendo Wii
systems.description: Nintendo's motion-controlled home console, notable for its innovative Wii Remote and massive mainstream appeal.

systems.name: Nintendo Wii U
systems.description: Nintendo's tablet-controller-equipped home console, featuring the GamePad with a built-in screen.

systems.name: Bandai WonderSwan
systems.description: Bandai's monochrome handheld console designed by the creator of the Game Boy, Gunpei Yokoi.

systems.name: Bandai WonderSwan Color
systems.description: Bandai's color upgrade to the WonderSwan handheld featuring a vibrant color screen.

systems.name: Sharp X68000
systems.description: Sharp's X68000, a powerful Japanese home computer beloved for near-perfect arcade ports.

systems.name: Microsoft Xbox
systems.description: Microsoft's original Xbox console, featuring a built-in hard drive and online gaming via Xbox Live.

systems.name: Microsoft Xbox 360
systems.description: Microsoft's seventh-generation console, home to Halo, Gears of War, and the modern Xbox Live service.

systems.name: Sinclair ZX Spectrum
systems.description: Sinclair's iconic 8-bit home computer, a cornerstone of British gaming culture through the 1980s.
